perturbation.original_text: GSEnergy agrees to maintain all Information in secrecy and confidence for a period of seven (7) years from the date of disclosure thereof, using the same degree of care as is used to protect its own Information.
perturbation.changed_text: GSEnergy agrees to maintain all Information in secrecy and confidence for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure thereof, using the same degree of care as is used to protect its own Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Massachusetts law, especially considering the context of 'proprietary and commercially sensitive information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law protects trade secrets, which often require protection for longer than one year to maintain their value and secrecy. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable for certain types of proprietary information, potentially undermining trade secret protection under M.G.L. c. 93 § 42.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from seven years to one year. The Law Explanation states that this one-year period may be deemed unreasonable for certain proprietary information, undermining trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' and refers to 'breach of a confidential relationship' as 'improper means' of acquiring a trade secret. By shortening the confidentiality period, the changed text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially facilitating misappropriation after the one-year period expires, thereby contradicting the intent of M.G.L. c. 93 § 42 to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: It will not constitute a violation of this Agreement for GSEnergy to disclose Information as required by a governmental body or a court of competent jurisdiction or as otherwise required by law, provided that the Company has been given notice of such requirement and been afforded a reasonable opportunity to contest it, if applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: It will not constitute a violation of this Agreement for GSEnergy to disclose Information as required by a governmental body or a court of competent jurisdiction or as otherwise required by law, without providing any notice to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the Company before disclosing information as required by law could violate legal precedent regarding the protection of confidential information and trade secrets, particularly if the Company could have taken steps to protect the information through legal challenges or protective orders.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), M.G.L. c. 93, §§ 42-42G
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), M.G.L. c. 93, §§ 42-42G, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are required to protect trade secrets. Disclosing information without notice to the Company, preventing them from taking legal action to protect their trade secrets, could be viewed as a failure to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, thereby jeopardizing trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for GSEnergy to notify the Company before disclosing information, which contradicts the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required by the Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this, stating that this removal could violate legal precedent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' which includes the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, potentially occurring if the Company is not notified and cannot take protective measures. The removal of the notification requirement directly undermines the Company's ability to protect its trade secrets, indicating a direct and serious contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, while facially permissible, could lead to a contradiction if the contract's provisions are significantly different from what Massachusetts law would allow or require, especially regarding trade secrets or contract interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: M.G.L. c. 93, § 42 (Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties are generally free to choose a governing law, if Delaware law provides significantly less protection for trade secrets compared to Massachusetts law (M.G.L. c. 93, § 42), it could effectively weaken protections initially intended by the agreement, especially if the agreement was crafted with Massachusetts law in mind. This could be an issue if the company is in Massachusetts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The change in the governing law from Massachusetts to Delaware could create a contradiction because Delaware law might offer less protection for trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, this could weaken the protections initially intended by the agreement, particularly if the company is based in Massachusetts. Scraped Law Snippet 1, M.G.L. c. 93, § 42, defines 'Improper means' and 'Misappropriation' related to trade secrets in Massachusetts. If Delaware law has a significantly weaker definition or enforcement regarding these concepts, the contract could be interpreted as contradicting the initial intent to protect trade secrets under Massachusetts law. This falls under Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction because changing the governing law results in terms that create a weaker protection, violating the initial agreement.

perturbation.original_text: All notices from GSEnergy hereunder shall be in writing and delivered to the attention of Mr. Michael J. Hager, New England Power Company, 25 Research Drive, Westborough, MA 01582. All notices from the Company hereunder shall be in writing and delivered to GSEnergy at the above address.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices from GSEnergy hereunder shall be delivered via telepathy. All notices from the Company hereunder shall be delivered via carrier pigeon.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying telepathy and carrier pigeon as the only acceptable methods of notice is not only impractical, but it effectively negates any reasonable expectation of actual notice, violating basic principles of contract law concerning adequate notification. It renders the contract unenforceable due to impossibility and lack of good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 106, Section 1-202 states that a person notifies or gives a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not such other person actually comes to know of it. Telepathy and carrier pigeon would not be considered a 'reasonable' method of notice under M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-202.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-202 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-202 Notice; Knowledge Section 1-202 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-202: Notice; Knowledge Section 1–202. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has ''notice'' of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) ''Knowledge'', actual knowledge. ''Knows'' has a corresponding meaning. (c) ''Discover'', ''learn'' or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person ''notifies'' or ''gives'' a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person ''receives'' a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the tr
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 1-202 Notice; Knowledge :: 2016 Massachusetts General Laws :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Massachusetts General Laws 2023 Mass. Gen. Laws 2022 Mass. Gen. Laws 2021 Mass. Gen. Laws 2016 Mass. Gen. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2016 Massachusetts General Laws PART I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT TITLE XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-202 Notice; Knowledge MA Gen L ch 1 § 1-202 (2016) What's This? [Text of section applicable as provided by 2013, 30, Sec. 116.] Section 1-202. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has ''notice'' of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) ''Knowledge'', actual knowledge. ''Knows'' has a corresponding meaning. (c) ''Discover'', ''learn'' or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person ''notifies'' or ''gives'' a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person ''receives'' a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies 'telepathy' and 'carrier pigeon' as methods for delivering notices. This contradicts Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 106, Section 1-202, which states that a person 'notifies' or 'gives' a notice by 'taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course.' The scraped law snippet 1 and 2 contains the same relevant text from Section 1-202 (d), supporting that the new methods are not reasonable. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists as the altered contract clause would not meet the legal standard of reasonable notification.
